rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Confidential Settlement Information In Petition For Special Needs Trust
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Defendants Uber Technologies, Inc, Rasier, LLC, and Rasier-CA, LLC ("Defendants") are parties to an action that has reached a settlement in Case No. CGC-19-574954, Moss v. Uber Technologies, Inc. et al. A Petition for Approval of Compromise of Claim or Action or Disposition of Proceeds of Judgment for Minor or a Person With a Disability, and a Motion to Seal Confidential Settlement Information are pending before a different department of this court, in that case number. Petitioner Carolyn Snipes is the Guardian ad Litem for the disabled adult plaintiff in that civil case. Pending in this department are Petitioner Snipes' Petition to Establish First Party Special Needs Trust, for Order to Execute Joinder Documents, and for Attorney's Fees (Probate Code sections 3600-3613), and Defendants' Motion to Seal. Defendants seek an order sealing (1) references to disbursement amounts from the settlement and (2) bond amounts that reveal the disbursement amounts from the settlement, contained in the Petition to Establish First Party Special Needs Trust, for Order to Execute Joinder Documents, and for Attorney's Fees, at specified page and line numbers of the Petition. The Motion to Seal is DENIED. A court may order that a record be filed under seal only if it expressly finds facts that establish all of the following: (1) an overriding interest exists that overcomes the right of public access to the record; (2) the overriding interest supports sealing the records; (3) a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; (4) the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and, (5) no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(d)(1)-(5); NBC Subsidiary (KNBC-TV), Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (Locke) (1999) 20 Cal.4th 1178, 1216-1218 ("NBC Subsidiary").) The party seeking the sealing of a record bears the burden of presenting information sufficient to identify the specific information claimed to be entitled to such treatment and the nature of the harm threatened by disclosure. (H.B. Fuller Co. v. Doe (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 879, 894.) The moving party must set forth specific facts in support of a motion to seal; statements that are too conclusory, abstract, or generic are not sufficient to satisfy the burden. (Id. at pp. 895-896.) [Part 1 of 3]

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Seal Confidential Settlement Information In Petition For Special Needs Trust (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] Defendants shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a hard-copy proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling prior to the hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the court day before the hearing. Remote appearance is available consistent with San Francisco Superior Court Local Rules, rule 14.1(D). To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 3 of 3]

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Seal Confidential Settlement Information In Petition For Special Needs Trust (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] The public's right to access to court records is fundamental to our constitutional system. (See NBC Subsidiary, supra, 20 Cal.4th 1216-1217 [First Amendment principles require that substantive courtroom proceedings in ordinary civil cases are "presumptively open"]; Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(c) [court records presumed to be open].) The right to access is heightened in probate proceedings, where broad notice and opportunity to object is fundamental. (See, e.g., Estate of Stevenson (2006) 141 Cal.App.4th 1074, 1087-88 ["Notice is the cornerstone of estate proceedings. [Citations.]"].) Notice is lacking when access to the document submitted for judicial approval is withheld from interested parties, effectively removing them from the decision-making process. An open discussion assumes access to the record. Defendants seek to seal the amounts funding the special needs trust and the amount of bond. Defendant's argument in the motion that "[t]he public will have its ordinary right of access to all other aspects of the Underlying Petition, as well as this Motion to Seal" does not ameliorate the loss of access. (Motion at 4:20-22.) Specifically, the court's consideration of the Petition to Establish First Party Special Needs Trust, for Order to Execute Joinder Documents, and for Attorney's Fees will require an open discussion of the information presented in the Petition, including the amount to be paid to the trust and the amount of bond, relative to the beneficiary's projected needs. (See Prob. C., section 3604, subd. (b).) Additionally, any future probate proceedings relative to the special needs trust will likely also require open discussion of this information, which will not be possible if the information is under sealed. (See Prob. C., sections 3602, 3604(a), 3611; see also Cal. Rules of Ct., rules 6.903(c), 7.903.) Defendants' reliance on the litigation parties' agreement to keep the settlement distribution amount confidential is misplaced, in this context. A special needs trust is subject to the continuing jurisdiction of the probate court, and is subject to court supervision to the extent the court determines necessary (see Prob Code section 3604, subd. (a) and (c).) This is the existential difference between "specific financial information, which is subject to a contractual duty not to disclose" (Motion at 4:15-16) in the civil cases Defendants cite, and financial information necessary to a probate court's continuing fiduciary oversight of a special needs trust. The litigation parties' interests do not override the public interest in open court records in special needs trust proceedings that are intended to be robust to ensure that the disabled beneficiary's interests are protected. Defendants have not presented specific facts showing that the litigation parties' or the beneficiary's privacy interests outweigh the public interest. (See Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 2.550(d)(2).) [Part 2 of 3]
